content:
# Genesis Chapter 48 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 28r–28v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_048_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_048_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_048_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_048_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`
**View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0053 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0054

---

*Joseph Brings His Sons to the Ailing Jacob (vv. 1–2)*

**48:1** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] after these things [אַחֲרֵי הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] that one said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to Joseph [לְיוֹסֵף]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — your father is ill [אָבִיךָ חֹלֶה] — and he took [וַיִּקַּח] his two sons with him [אֶת-שְׁנֵי בָנָיו עִמּוֹ] — Manasseh [מְנַשֶּׁה] and Ephraim [וְאֶת-אֶפְרָיִם].

**48:2** And one told Jacob [וַיַּגֵּד לְיַעֲקֹב] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — your son Joseph has come to you [בִּנְךָ יוֹסֵף בָּא אֵלֶיךָ] — and Israel strengthened himself [וַיִּתְחַזֵּק יִשְׂרָאֵל] and sat up [וַיֵּשֶׁב] upon the bed [עַל-הַמִּטָּה].

---

*Jacob Recounts the Covenant at Luz — The Adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh (vv. 3–7)*

**48:3** And Jacob said to Joseph [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל-יוֹסֵף]: El Shaddai appeared to me [אֵל שַׁדַּי נִרְאָה-אֵלַי] at Luz [בְּלוּז] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] and he blessed me [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתִי].

**48:4** And he said to me [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלַי]: Behold [הִנְנִי] — I will make you fruitful [מַפְרְךָ] and multiply you [וְהִרְבִּיתִיךָ] and I will make you into an assembly of peoples [וּנְתַתִּיךָ לִקְהַל עַמִּים] — and I will give [וְנָתַתִּי] this land [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] to your seed after you [לְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ] as a perpetual possession [אַחֻזַּת עוֹלָם].

**48:5** And now [וְעַתָּה] — your two sons [שְׁנֵי בָנֶיךָ] who were born to you [הַנּוֹלָדִים לְךָ] in the land of Egypt [בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] before my coming to you [עַד-בֹּאִי אֵלֶיךָ מִצְרַיְמָה] — they are mine [לִי-הֵם] — Ephraim and Manasseh [אֶפְרַיִם וּמְנַשֶּׁה] — as Reuben and Simeon they shall be mine [כִּרְאוּבֵן וְשִׁמְעוֹן יִהְיוּ-לִי].

**48:6** But your offspring [וּמוֹלַדְתְּךָ] that you have fathered after them [אֲשֶׁר-הוֹלַדְתָּ אַחֲרֵיהֶם] — they shall be yours [לְךָ יִהְיוּ] — by the name of their brothers they shall be called [עַל שֵׁם אֲחֵיהֶם יִקָּרְאוּ] in their inheritance [בְּנַחֲלָתָם].

**48:7** And as for me [וַאֲנִי] — when I came from Paddan [בְּבֹאִי מִפַּדָּן] — Rachel died upon me [מֵתָה עָלַי רָחֵל] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] on the way [בַּדֶּרֶךְ] when there was yet some distance of land [בְּעוֹד כִּבְרַת-אֶרֶץ] to come to Ephrath [לָבוֹא אֶפְרָתָה] — and I buried her there [וָאֶקְבְּרֶהָ שָׁם] on the road to Ephrath [בְּדֶרֶךְ אֶפְרָת] — that is Bethlehem [הִוא בֵּית לָחֶם].

---

*Jacob's Failing Eyes — "Who Are These?" (vv. 8–11)*

**48:8** And Israel saw [וַיַּרְא יִשְׂרָאֵל] the sons of Joseph [אֶת-בְּנֵי יוֹסֵף] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Who are these [מִי-אֵלֶּה]?

**48:9** And Joseph said to his father [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אָבִיו]: They are my sons [בָּנַי הֵם] whom God has given me [אֲשֶׁר-נָתַן-לִי אֱלֹהִים] here [בָּזֶה] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Bring them near to me [קָחֶם-נָא אֵלַי] and I will bless them [וַאֲבָרֲכֵם].

**48:10** And the eyes of Israel were heavy [וְעֵינֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כָּבְדוּ] from age [מִזֹּקֶן] — he was not able to see [לֹא יוּכַל לִרְאוֹת] — and he drew them near to him [וַיַּגֵּשׁ אֹתָם אֵלָיו] and he kissed them [וַיִּשַּׁק לָהֶם] and embraced them [וַיְחַבֵּק לָהֶם].

**48:11** And Israel said to Joseph [וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-יוֹסֵף]: I had not thought [לֹא פִלָּלְתִּי] to see your face [רְאוֹת פָּנֶיךָ] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — God has let me see [הֶרְאָה אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים] even your seed [גַּם אֶת-זַרְעֶךָ].

---

*The Cross-Handed Blessing — Ephraim Before Manasseh (vv. 12–20)*

**48:12** And Joseph brought them out [וַיּוֹצֵא יוֹסֵף אֹתָם] from with his knees [מֵעִם בִּרְכָּיו] and he bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ] with his face to the ground [לְאַפָּיו אָרְצָה].

**48:13** And Joseph took the two of them [וַיִּקַּח יוֹסֵף אֶת-שְׁנֵיהֶם] — Ephraim in his right hand [אֶת-אֶפְרַיִם בִּימִינוֹ] to Israel's left [מִשְּׂמֹאל יִשְׂרָאֵל] — and Manasseh in his left hand [וְאֶת-מְנַשֶּׁה בִשְׂמֹאלוֹ] to Israel's right [מִימִין יִשְׂרָאֵל] — and he brought them near to him [וַיַּגֵּשׁ אֵלָיו].

**48:14** And Israel stretched out [וַיִּשְׁלַח יִשְׂרָאֵל] his right hand [אֶת-יְמִינוֹ] and placed it [וַיָּשֶׁת] on the head of Ephraim [עַל-רֹאשׁ אֶפְרָיִם] — and he was the younger [וְהוּא הַצָּעִיר] — and his left hand on the head of Manasseh [וְשְׂמֹאלוֹ עַל-רֹאשׁ מְנַשֶּׁה] — he crossed his hands [שִׂכֵּל אֶת-יָדָיו] — for Manasseh was the firstborn [כִּי מְנַשֶּׁה הַבְּכוֹר].

**48:15** And he blessed Joseph [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֶת-יוֹסֵף] and he said [וַיֹּאמֵר]: The God before whom my fathers walked [הָאֱלֹהִים אֲשֶׁר הִתְהַלְּכוּ אֲבֹתַי לְפָנָיו] — Abraham and Isaac [אַבְרָהָם וְיִצְחָק] — the God who has been my shepherd [הָאֱלֹהִים הָרֹעֶה אֹתִי] since my birth until this day [מֵעוֹדִי עַד-הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] —

**48:16** the angel who has redeemed me from all evil [הַמַּלְאָךְ הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי מִכָּל-רָע] — may he bless the lads [יְבָרֵךְ אֶת-הַנְּעָרִים] — and let my name be called upon them [וְיִקָּרֵא בָהֶם שְׁמִי] and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac [וְשֵׁם אֲבֹתַי אַבְרָהָם וְיִצְחָק] — and may they swarm [וְיִדְגּוּ] into a multitude in the midst of the earth [לָרֹב בְּקֶרֶב הָאָרֶץ].

**48:17** And Joseph saw [וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף] that his father had placed [כִּי-יָשִׁית אָבִיו] his right hand [יְמִינוֹ] on the head of Ephraim [עַל-רֹאשׁ אֶפְרָיִם] — and it was evil in his eyes [וַיֵּרַע בְּעֵינָיו] — and he supported [וַיִּתְמֹךְ] his father's hand [יַד-אָבִיו] to move it [לְהָסִיר אֹתָהּ] from upon the head of Ephraim [מֵעַל רֹאשׁ-אֶפְרַיִם] to upon the head of Manasseh [עַל-רֹאשׁ מְנַשֶּׁה].

**48:18** And Joseph said to his father [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אָבִיו]: Not so, my father [לֹא-כֵן אָבִי] — for this one is the firstborn [כִּי-זֶה הַבְּכֹר] — place your right hand upon his head [שִׂים יְמִינְךָ עַל-רֹאשׁוֹ].

**48:19** And his father refused [וַיְמָאֵן אָבִיו] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I know, my son, I know [יָדַעְתִּי בְנִי יָדַעְתִּי] — he also shall become a people [גַּם-הוּא יִהְיֶה-לְּעָם] and he also shall be great [וְגַם-הוּא יִגְדָּל] — but his younger brother shall be greater than he [וְאוּלָם אָחִיו הַקָּטֹן יִגְדַּל מִמֶּנּוּ] — and his seed shall become the fullness of the nations [וְזַרְעוֹ יִהְיֶה מְלֹא-הַגּוֹיִם].

**48:20** And he blessed them on that day [וַיְבָרֲכֵם בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא] saying [לֵאמֹר]: By you [בְּךָ] Israel will pronounce blessings [יְבָרֵךְ יִשְׂרָאֵל] saying [לֵאמֹר]: May God make you like Ephraim and like Manasseh [יְשִׂמְךָ אֱלֹהִים כְּאֶפְרַיִם וְכִמְנַשֶּׁה] — and he set Ephraim before Manasseh [וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת-אֶפְרַיִם לִפְנֵי מְנַשֶּׁה].

---

*Jacob's Final Words to Joseph — The Portion of Shechem (vv. 21–22)*

**48:21** And Israel said to Joseph [וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-יוֹסֵף]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — I am about to die [אָנֹכִי מֵת] — but God will be with you [וְהָיָה אֱלֹהִים עִמָּכֶם] and bring you back [וְהֵשִׁיב אֶתְכֶם] to the land of your fathers [אֶל-אֶרֶץ אֲבֹתֵיכֶם].

**48:22** And I — I have given you [וַאֲנִי נָתַתִּי לְךָ] one portion [שְׁכֶם אַחַד] above your brothers [עַל-אַחֶיךָ] — which I took [אֲשֶׁר לָקַחְתִּי] from the hand of the Amorite [מִיַּד הָאֱמֹרִי] with my sword [בְּחַרְבִּי] and with my bow [וּבְקַשְׁתִּי].

---

## Philological Notes

**1. vayithazzeq (48:2) — "he strengthened himself" [וַיִּתְחַזֵּק]**
The verb hazaq in the Hitpael (reflexive-intensive) form denotes self-summoning of inner strength — "he pulled himself together," "he exerted himself." The root chazaq fundamentally means "to be strong, firm"; in the Hitpael it describes an act of the will against physical weakness. The subject shifts tellingly from "Jacob" (v. 1, the personal name) to "Israel" (v. 2, the covenant name) at the moment of exertion, suggesting that Jacob acts here in his patriarchal capacity. The Hitpael of chazaq recurs in contexts of dying or ill figures rousing themselves for a final act (cf. 1 Kgs 20:22; the verb echoes the command to "be strong" in leadership contexts, Deut 31:6). The translation "strengthened himself" preserves the reflexive force exactly.

**2. El Shaddai (48:3) — [אֵל שַׁדַּי]**
Jacob's recollection of the Luz theophany invokes the divine name El Shaddai without the article — the proper name form. The appearance at Luz (= Bethel) matches Gen 35:9–12, where God appeared to Jacob after his return from Paddan-aram and renewed the Abrahamic covenant of fruitfulness and land. El Shaddai is the characteristic patriarchal name for God (Exod 6:3: "I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai"), associated specifically with the fertility-and-increase aspect of the covenant promises. The name is preserved untranslated per project convention; rendering it "God Almighty" (LXX: ὁ θεός μου) would flatten the archaic covenant-name character.

**3. achuzzat olam (48:4) — "perpetual possession" [אַחֻזַּת עוֹלָם]**
The construct phrase achuzzah (from 'achaz, "to seize, hold") + olam ("age, eternity, perpetuity") appears in its fullest formulaic form here. Achuzzah is the technical term for a heritable, inalienable landholding — used for the cave of Machpelah (Gen 23:4, 9, 20) and for tribal allotments in the Promised Land (Lev 25:34; Num 27:7). The modifier olam intensifies the permanence: this is not a leasehold but an eternal grant. "Perpetual possession" captures both the legal weight of achuzzah and the temporal scope of olam. The LXX renders εἰς κατάσχεσιν αἰώνιον, preserving both components.

**4. sikkel et-yadav (48:14) — "he crossed his hands" [שִׂכֵּל אֶת-יָדָיו]**
The verb sakhal in the Piel is rare and much discussed. The root (s-k-l) carries connotations of understanding, acting with insight, or acting purposefully (cf. 1 Sam 18:14, "David acted with prudence"; Prov 16:20). In the Piel here it describes a deliberate, knowing gesture — Jacob crosses his hands with intent, not by accident or infirmity. The adverb "deliberately" (or "with insight") is latent in the verb itself. The narrative carefully excludes any possibility that the crossed hands are an error of his failing eyes (v. 10): the text states he placed the right on Ephraim "for he was the younger" (כִּי הַצָּעִיר), and the subsequent exchange (vv. 17–19) confirms Jacob's consciousness. "He crossed his hands" renders the physical act; the deliberateness is carried by the Piel stem and contextually by vv. 17–19.

**5. ha-mal'akh ha-go'el oti mikkol-ra (48:16) — "the angel who has redeemed me from all evil" [הַמַּלְאָךְ הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי מִכָּל-רָע]**
This phrase is theologically dense. The participial construction ha-go'el oti ("the one-who-redeems me") applies the go'el ("kinsman-redeemer") concept to a divine being. Go'el denotes the family member who acts on behalf of a relative in legal or mortal crisis — who redeems enslaved kin, avengers blood, preserves the family inheritance (Lev 25:25; Ruth 3:12). Jacob applies this most intimate social concept to the angel, implying a familial-protective relationship between the divine messenger and Jacob. The phrase stands as the third member of a threefold divine invocation in vv. 15–16: (1) the God before whom the fathers walked (transcendent covenant God), (2) the God who shepherded Jacob all his life (providential companion), (3) the angel-redeemer from all evil (personal deliverer). The syntactic structure of the blessing places all three as the implicit subject of "may he bless" — the three are addressed as one. The christological reading of this angel (identifying him with the one who wrestled with Jacob at Peniel, Gen 32) has a long exegetical tradition; the translation renders literally without prejudging the referent.

**6. yidgu larov (48:16) — "may they swarm into a multitude" [וְיִדְגּוּ לָרֹב]**
The verb dagah (from dag, "fish") means literally "to multiply like fish" — an onomatopoeic fertility image. It appears only here in the Hebrew Bible as a verb (the noun dag, "fish," is common). The image evokes the swarms of fish in water, the teeming fecundity of the sea, and connects with the Priestly creation mandate to "fill" the earth (Gen 1:22, 28). The fish-multiplication image is apt for sons of a man whose name Ephraim (from parah, "to be fruitful") already carries fertility connotations. "May they swarm into a multitude" preserves the unusual verb; alternatives like "may they multiply" lose the zoological specificity.

**7. melo ha-goyim (48:19) — "the fullness of the nations" [מְלֹא-הַגּוֹיִם]**
The construct phrase melo ("fullness, abundance") + ha-goyim ("the nations") is uniquely applied to Ephraim's seed. The word melo (from male', "to fill") denotes a completeness-of-quantity. The referent has been interpreted variously: (a) Ephraim's descendants will fill up the quota of the nations (missionary/universal reading); (b) Ephraim will become a great confederation comparable to multiple nations; (c) Ephraim's name will be great among/throughout all the nations. The LXX renders πλῆθος ἐθνῶν ("multitude of nations"), reflecting meaning (b). The phrase is applied in later tradition specifically to the Northern Kingdom, of which Ephraim was the dominant tribe. The translation renders literally: "the fullness of the nations."

**8. kivrat-ha'arets (48:7) — "some distance of land" [כִּבְרַת-אֶרֶץ]**
The phrase kivrat-ha'arets is a hapax in the specific form used here; the related kivrah appears also in 2 Kgs 5:19 ("go in peace... and he went from him a little way"). The term describes an indeterminate short distance — a "stretch of land," a "measure of ground." The translation "some distance of land" preserves the vagueness that is inherent in the Hebrew; rendering it as a specific measure (a league, a mile) imports false precision. The phrase anchors Rachel's death topographically — near enough to Bethlehem to name it, but not yet arrived. Jacob's parenthetical memory of Rachel here (inserted between the adoption decree of v. 6 and the blessing scene of vv. 8ff.) is emotionally charged: he is adopting Rachel's grandsons precisely as he recalls her death giving birth to one of their fathers.

**9. lo-khillelti (48:11) — "I had not thought" [לֹא פִלָּלְתִּי]**
The verb palal in the Piel here (pillelti) means "to reckon, calculate, think it possible" — not the usual meaning of palal ("to pray, intercede") that dominates its Hitpael form (hitpallel). The phrase lo-pillelti re'ot panekha ("I had not counted on seeing your face") is a statement of resigned hopelessness turned to wonder. The rare Piel of palal in this sense of "to reckon, expect" appears to convey an act of calculation — Jacob had mentally written off the possibility. The translation "I had not thought to see your face" renders this nuance; "I had not expected" would also be acceptable.

**10. shechem echad (48:22) — "one portion" [שְׁכֶם אַחַד]**
The word shechem is a deliberate double entendre. As a common noun, shechem means "shoulder, slope" (metaphorically, a "portion, share" of land). As a proper noun, Shechem is the city in Canaan associated with Abraham's first altar (Gen 12:6), Jacob's land purchase (Gen 33:19), the Dinah incident (Gen 34), and Joseph's burial (Josh 24:32). Jacob's statement "I give you one shechem above your brothers" activates both meanings simultaneously — a wordplay that can only be reproduced in translation with a note. The reference to taking it "from the hand of the Amorite with my sword and my bow" is puzzling, since no such conquest appears in the Genesis narrative (Gen 34 describes the sons' violence, not Jacob's, and Jacob explicitly dissociates himself from it in Gen 34:30 and 49:5–7). This is either a reference to a tradition not preserved in Genesis, a poetic way of expressing the legal right purchased in Gen 33:19, or a dying man's reimagining of events from a heroic angle. The translation preserves the ambiguity.

**11. vayme'en aviv (48:19) — "and his father refused" [וַיְמָאֵן אָבִיו]**
The verb ma'en ("to refuse") is a strong word — it is used of Pharaoh's repeated refusals in Exodus (Exod 7:14; 8:2; 9:2) and of Joseph's refusal of Potiphar's wife (Gen 39:8). Jacob's use of the same verb constitutes an act of sovereign will: the dying patriarch refuses the correction of the vizier of Egypt. The syntactic economy is sharp — the refusal is stated, then the doubled "I know, my son, I know" (yada'ti beni yada'ti) follows immediately, transforming resistance into pedagogical explanation. The double "I know" mirrors the double-name vocatives of divine address elsewhere in Genesis (22:11; 46:2), here used by the patriarch himself to command attention.

**12. yarev beineihem (48:17, implied) / ra ba-einav (48:17) — "it was evil in his eyes" [וַיֵּרַע בְּעֵינָיו]**
The idiom ra be-einav ("it was evil/displeasing in his eyes") is the standard Biblical Hebrew construction for disapproval or displeasure (cf. Gen 21:11–12; 38:10; Num 11:1). The eyes function as the organ of moral and aesthetic judgment in Hebrew idiom — what one "sees as bad" is what one disapproves of. The translation renders literally: "it was evil in his eyes," preserving the idiom rather than substituting "he was displeased." The literalism highlights the irony: it is Joseph who sees correctly (physically) but Jacob whose eyes see truly (theologically), reversing the visual motif that pervades the chapter (v. 10: Jacob's heavy eyes; v. 8: "who are these?").

---

## LXX Comparison Table (MT vs. Codex Vaticanus)

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|---------------------|-----------------|------|
| 48:3 | "El Shaddai appeared to me at Luz" [אֵל שַׁדַּי נִרְאָה-אֵלַי בְּלוּז] | "my God appeared to me at Luz" (ὁ θεός μου ὤφθη μοι ἐν Λουζα) | LXX renders El Shaddai as ὁ θεός μου ("my God"), losing the divine name; MT preserves archaic covenant name |
| 48:4 | "an assembly of peoples [לִקְהַל עַמִּים]" | "gatherings of nations" (εἰς συναγωγὰς ἐθνῶν) | LXX pluralizes both nouns; MT has singular qahal |
| 48:4 | "perpetual possession [אַחֻזַּת עוֹלָם]" | "eternal possession" (εἰς κατάσχεσιν αἰώνιον) | Close agreement; both preserve the eternity qualifier |
| 48:5 | "Ephraim and Manasseh [אֶפְרַיִם וּמְנַשֶּׁה]" (Ephraim first) | "Ephraim and Manasseh" (Εφραιμ και Μανασση) | Both list Ephraim first in v. 5, anticipating the reversal; notable that MT has Ephraim first even in Jacob's adoption speech |
| 48:7 | "some distance of land [כִּבְרַת-אֶרֶץ]" | "at the hippodrome" (ἐν τῷ ἱπποδρόμῳ) | LXX rendering is obscure; possibly a geographic gloss equating kivrat with a known location near Bethlehem, or a mistranslation; MT idiom not reproduced |
| 48:10 | "the eyes of Israel were heavy from age [כָּבְדוּ מִזֹּקֶן]" | "the eyes of Israel were dim from old age" (ἐβαρύθησαν ἀπὸ τοῦ γήρους) | Close agreement; LXX βαρύθησαν reproduces the "heaviness" metaphor of MT kavad |
| 48:14 | "he crossed his hands [שִׂכֵּל אֶת-יָדָיו]" | "he crossed his hands" (ἐναλλάξ τὰς χεῖρας) | LXX ἐναλλάξ ("alternating, crossing") accurately renders the deliberate gesture; good agreement |
| 48:15 | "the God who has been my shepherd [הָאֱלֹהִים הָרֹעֶה אֹתִי]" | "the God who feeds me" (ὁ θεὸς ὁ τρέφων με) | LXX uses τρέφω ("nourish, feed") rather than "shepherd" (ποιμαίνω); softens the pastoral metaphor |
| 48:16 | "the angel who has redeemed me [הַמַּלְאָךְ הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי]" | "the angel who delivers me" (ὁ ἄγγελος ὁ ῥυόμενός με) | LXX ῥύομαι ("deliver, rescue") for go'el; loses the kinsman-redeemer legal nuance of the Hebrew root |
| 48:16 | "may they swarm [וְיִדְגּוּ] into a multitude" | "may they multiply [πληθυνθείησαν] into a multitude" | LXX uses standard multiply verb, losing the fish-swarming imagery of dagah |
| 48:19 | "the fullness of the nations [מְלֹא-הַגּוֹיִם]" | "multitude of nations" (πλῆθος ἐθνῶν) | LXX πλῆθος ("multitude") for melo ("fullness"); interpretive rather than literal |
| 48:20 | "May God make you like Ephraim and like Manasseh" | "May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh" | Agreement in substance; LXX lacks the second כְּ (like) before Manasseh in some recensions |
| 48:22 | "one portion [שְׁכֶם אַחַד]... which I took from the Amorite" | "Shechem... which I took from the Amorites" | LXX reads Σικιμα (Shechem the city), making the proper-noun reading explicit and eliminating the wordplay; MT's double meaning is flattened |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 48 is a tightly unified scene of patriarchal succession and covenantal redistribution, organized around a single axis: the deliberate reversal of primogeniture.

**Three Interlocking Movements:**

*Movement 1 — The Adoption and Its Grounding (vv. 1–7):* The chapter opens in medias res — Jacob is already ill, already near death. The scene is activated by news: "your father is ill." Joseph comes with both his sons, an act of expectation; he anticipates a deathbed blessing. Jacob, however, moves first with a formal legal action: adoption. The adoption declaration (vv. 5–6) is juridically precise — "they are mine, as Reuben and Simeon." By naming the firstborn (Reuben) and second-born (Simeon) as the parallel, Jacob implies that Manasseh and Ephraim will be full tribal members in the inheritance of Israel, not merely Joseph's sons. The theological grounding of this adoption is Jacob's retelling of the El Shaddai theophany at Luz (Bethel), where the covenant of fruitfulness and perpetual possession was confirmed. The adoption is thus not arbitrary but an act of covenantal transmission — El Shaddai's promise flows through Jacob directly to Ephraim and Manasseh, bypassing the Egyptian context of their birth entirely.

The interpolated memory of Rachel (v. 7) functions as a parenthetical explanation for the adoption of Joseph's sons: because Rachel died before she could produce further sons, Jacob adopts her Egyptian-born grandsons as her proxy-heirs, restoring to Rachel's line the tribal weight that her early death would otherwise have diminished. This single verse quietly reveals Jacob's motive and bears enormous emotional weight.

*Movement 2 — The Cross-Handed Blessing (vv. 8–20):* The central scene is staged with deliberate drama. Jacob's failing eyes (v. 10) create dramatic tension: he cannot see whom he touches. Joseph positions the boys accordingly — Manasseh to Jacob's right, Ephraim to Jacob's left (v. 13). But Jacob crosses his hands (v. 14), placing the right (the dominant, blessing hand) on Ephraim the younger. The verb sikkel (Piel) marks the act as deliberate and knowing: this is not confusion but intention. The blessing itself (vv. 15–16) is the theological climax of the chapter — a threefold invocation of God that moves from transcendence (the God before whom Abraham and Isaac walked) to immanence (the shepherding God of Jacob's own lifetime) to intimacy (the angel-redeemer who personally delivered Jacob from evil). The three forms of divine address constitute a miniature theology of the patriarchal God — cosmic covenant-maker, providential shepherd, personal kinsman.

Joseph's protest (vv. 17–18) triggers Jacob's climactic declaration: "I know, my son, I know" (v. 19). The doubled acknowledgment ("I know... I know") is the rhetorical counterpart to Jacob's deliberate hand-crossing — he insists, twice, on full cognizance. Manasseh will be great; Ephraim will be greater; and the blessing formula will become Israel's standard for invoking the highest blessing (v. 20: "May God make you like Ephraim and like Manasseh"). The reversal of v. 20 — "he set Ephraim before Manasseh" — enacts narratively what the hand-crossing enacted physically.

*Movement 3 — Final Words and the Shechem Bequest (vv. 21–22):* The chapter closes with two distinct final statements to Joseph. Verse 21 is a theological assurance: "God will be with you" — echoing the divine promise of Gen 46:4 and anticipating Exodus. Verse 22 is a personal bequest, unparalleled elsewhere in Genesis: Jacob gives Joseph one shechem (portion/Shechem) above his brothers, taken from the Amorite. The double meaning of shechem (shoulder-portion and city name) creates a compressed figure — Jacob gives Joseph both a superior share of the inheritance and a specific geographic anchor for his tribe in Canaan. The claim to have taken Shechem "with my sword and my bow" stands in apparent tension with Gen 34:30 and 49:5–7, where Jacob dissociates himself from his sons' violence; the tension may reflect a distinct tradition or Jacob's dying reconstitution of events into a heroic register appropriate for a patriarchal testament.

**Theological Unity:** The chapter's primary theological movement is the transfer of covenant blessing across generational and conventional lines. Three reversals are enacted: (1) Ephraim the younger is blessed above Manasseh the elder; (2) the Egyptian-born sons of Joseph become tribal ancestors of Israel; (3) Rachel's diminished line is restored to full tribal standing. All three reversals are traced explicitly to divine initiative — Jacob acts under the authority of El Shaddai's original covenant (v. 3), in continuity with the God who shepherded him his whole life (v. 15), empowered by the angel-redeemer who delivered him from all evil (v. 16). The chapter thus demonstrates that Israel's tribal structure is not the product of natural birth-order or human preference but of deliberate, theologically grounded reversal — the pattern of the younger over the elder that runs from Abel/Cain through Isaac/Ishmael through Jacob/Esau and now reaches its culmination in the double reversal of Joseph's sons.
